text: Throughout this audio tour, you’ll be hearing the voices of the officers and crew who served on Pampanito and other US submarines during World War II.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Deck 00:00:00.200–00:00:09.450 (Narrator)

text: We interrupt this program to bring you a special news bulletin. The Japanese have attacked Pearl Harbor, Hawaii, by air, President Roosevelt has just announced.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Deck 00:00:09.450–00:00:23.959 (Speaker 1)

text: President Franklin D. Roosevelt’s address to Congress, December 8th, 1941.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Deck 00:00:23.960–00:00:29.220 (Narrator)

text: Yesterday, December 7th, 1941, a date which will live in infamy, United States of America was suddenly and deliberately attacked by naval and air forces of the Empire of Japan. The attack yesterday-
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Deck 00:00:29.220–00:00:53.960 (Franklin D. Roosevelt)

text: Captain Edward L. Beach, naval historian, novelist, and veteran submariner.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Deck 00:00:53.960–00:00:59.200 (Narrator)

text: In response to the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor, the United States began a massive submarine-building program. The United States ship Pampanito was built in 1943 at the Portsmouth Navy Yard in New Hampshire. The role of US submarines was to cut off the flow of raw materials to Japan. The submarine fleet was instrumental in the Allied victory in the Pacific, far beyond its cost to the United States. Today, the Pampanito is being preserved as a memorial to the submarine service of the US Navy, which we proudly call the Silent Service. Let’s head below now to learn more about life aboard a wartime submarine.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Deck 00:00:59.200–00:01:42.580 (Edward L. Beach)

text: We knew that we would be going into the Pacific. We didn’t know where our, uh, war patrols would take us.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Deck 00:01:42.580–00:01:50.380 (Speaker 4)

text: Our survival rate was the least of, uh, any group, Army, Navy, or Marine Corps. We had about twenty-four percent casualties on people that actually went on patrol.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Deck 00:01:50.380–00:02:10.380 (Speaker 5)

text: It smelled of oil.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:00:00.280–00:00:01.800 (Robert Bennett)

text: Diesel oil. All of your clothes, the whole ship smells like diesel fuel, wherever I was. They knew a submarine sailor by the smell.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:00:01.800–00:00:09.500 (Speaker 1)

text: Submarines are tight quarters, and we all lived together, and-
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:00:09.500–00:00:12.440 (Robert Bennett)

text: I slept in the aft torpedo room, and if you couldn’t get to sleep, the rocking, the movement of the boat would put you to sleep.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:00:12.440–00:00:21.160 (Speaker 2)

text: And I had as my bedfellows, a torpedo on one side and a torpedo above me.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:00:21.160–00:00:27.340 (Speaker 1)

text: Torpedo man First Class, Robert Bennett.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:00:27.340–00:00:30.040 (Narrator)

text: We had two kinds of torpedoes. The steam torpedoes, they’re very fast.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:00:30.040–00:00:35.260 (Robert Bennett)

text: The torpedoes you see here are steam torpedoes. They’re called Mark 14s, and had two speed settings, thirty-one and forty-six knots.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:00:35.260–00:00:44.240 (Narrator)

text: The others were what they call Mark 18s. They were electric. The advantage to those was there wasn’t any bubbles from the wake when they were running, and they were quiet, but they were slow.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:00:44.240–00:00:55.560 (Robert Bennett)

text: The Mark 18’s top speed was twenty-nine knots. Look at the steel skids cradling the torpedoes on either side of this room.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:00:55.560–00:01:06.110 (Narrator)

text: They were on what we call skids on a track.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:01:06.110–00:01:09.620 (Robert Bennett)

text: Bennett describes how torpedoes were loaded.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:01:09.620–00:01:12.000 (Narrator)

text: Take some guys’ bunks off and pull the skid out until it lined up with the tube, and that’d take about three guys to do that.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:01:12.000–00:01:20.210 (Robert Bennett)

text: Take a look at the four large bronze torpedo tubes.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:01:20.210–00:01:23.900 (Narrator)

text: We had six tubes in the forward room and four tubes in the aft room. It was twenty-four torpedoes.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:01:23.900–00:01:30.040 (Robert Bennett)

text: Quartermaster Third Class, Gordon Hopper.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:01:30.040–00:01:32.240 (Narrator)

text: The torpedoes did not explode on a timer mechanism, but they had a, what we call an impeller, a little propeller in the nose of the torpedo that had to make a certain number of revolutions before the torpedo would be armed.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:01:32.240–00:01:45.980 (Gordon Hopper)

text: Ready to drop.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:01:45.980–00:01:47.560 (Speaker 5)

text: After the tube was loaded and the breach or inner door sealed, seawater was flooded into the tube until the pressure inside the tube equaled the sea pressure outside.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:01:47.560–00:01:58.000 (Narrator)

text: You could vary the depth that they would run and also the angle they would assume after they came out of the tube.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:01:58.000–00:02:04.860 (Gordon Hopper)

text: Fire seven.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:02:04.860–00:02:05.120 (Speaker 5)

text: Compressed air was used to actually push the torpedo out of the tube, but the torpedo’s own drive mechanism then took over to propel it on its way.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:02:05.120–00:02:14.300 (Narrator)

text: When it left the tube, there was a big shot of air behind it, and there was a big thunk, you know. You could feel it all over the submarine. You knew that they fired.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:02:14.300–00:02:23.620 (Robert Bennett)

text: Hot straight for.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:02:23.620–00:02:25.800 (Speaker 5)

text: We’ll work our way from here, the stern, forward to the bow or front of the boat. Look to your left. This side of the boat is known as the port side. The right side is called the starboard side. Just before you leave this compartment, to your left, one of the four heads or toilets for the crew of up to eighty-five men.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:02:25.800–00:02:45.440 (Narrator)

text: Sometimes you have to wait. That’s all there is to it.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Torpedo Room 00:02:45.440–00:02:48.299 (Speaker 1)

text: You’re looking at the propulsion control stand.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Maneuvering Room 00:00:01.279–00:00:04.000 (Narrator)

text: In the maneuvering room. Well, that’s where they control the engine speed and the load on the engine. This is where speed changes and direction changes of the propellers take place.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Maneuvering Room 00:00:04.000–00:00:14.199 (Subvet 1)

text: These levers were used to switch the electricity from the submarine’s generators to charge the batteries or to power the main propulsion motors. The submarine’s electric motors drove the twin propellers. Electrician’s Mate O.D. Hawkins:
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Maneuvering Room 00:00:14.199–00:00:28.819 (Narrator)

text: You have, uh, two men that are on the main control cubicle, a port operator and a starboard operator. The signal comes from the bridge on speeds like one-third forward, one-third reverse, charge the batteries. Control. All ahead, one-third. All ahead, one-third, aye. So we had control of the speed of the engines, how the, uh, electricity was taken from the generators, either to the batteries or to the main motors.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Maneuvering Room 00:00:28.819–00:00:58.959 (O.D. Hawkins)

text: The submarine was always operated by electrical power. The diesel engine generators powered the motors when the sub was on the surface. It ran on storage battery power when it was submerged.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Maneuvering Room 00:00:58.959–00:01:10.519 (Narrator)

text: Being a, uh, battery-powered submarine, diesel battery, we had to surface every night and charge batteries.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Maneuvering Room 00:01:10.519–00:01:20.020 (O.D. Hawkins)

text: The fastest the boat could go submerged was about nine knots, but this would drain the batteries in less than an hour. At slower speeds, she could go farther. Look to your left. Behind the narrow door is the tiny office for the electrical officer.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Maneuvering Room 00:01:20.020–00:01:39.079 (Narrator)

text: On either side of this room are two of the submarine’s four main engines. When she was running on the surface, Pampanito’s maximum speed was twenty-one knots, or about twenty-four miles per hour. And when the engines were running, this room was one of the loudest places on the submarine.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:00:00.100–00:00:23.980 (Narrator)

text: Submarines are noisy.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:00:23.980–00:00:25.759 (Joe Eichner)

text: The noise level at that time was so high, you basically couldn’t hear anybody talk. We did everything by signals, everything.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:00:25.760–00:00:35.600 (Chester Biankowski)

text: The only time the noise stopped is when the engines were shut down. This had to happen in order for the submarine to dive.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:00:35.600–00:00:45.620 (Narrator)

text: The engines we had was probably the best submarine engine anybody could ever build.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:00:45.620–00:00:50.260 (Chester Biankowski)

text: Motor Machinist Chester Biankowski.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:00:50.260–00:00:52.940 (Narrator)

text: Fairbanks-Morse, thirty-eight D, eight and eight. They were ten-cylinder. Opposed piston, we had two crankshafts, one on the upper crank and one on the lower crank, naturally. And the pistons would come together, and the pressure of the air being trapped in there generated so much heat that it ignited the diesel fuel.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:00:52.940–00:01:12.340 (Chester Biankowski)

text: At times, that heat also circulated throughout the submarine.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:01:12.340–00:01:17.080 (Narrator)

text: I used to absolutely detest going down on a dive after we’d been running on the surface for six or eight hours with all four main engines online.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:01:17.080–00:01:27.760 (George Moffett)

text: Radar Technician George Moffett.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:01:27.760–00:01:30.340 (Narrator)

text: Because by then, the boat was hot, the engines were hot, and with no ventilation, guess where all that engine heat came? Throughout all the rest of the boat, and it would get hot.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:01:30.340–00:01:44.060 (George Moffett)

text: Look toward the middle of the room. You’ll see a ladder leading up to a deck access hatch. Just forward of this is another round opening. This is one of the hull valves that supplied the boat’s air via the main induction.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:01:44.060–00:01:59.100 (Narrator)

text: The main induction is a monstrous hole way up front of the ship at the bridge that drew in air through a thirty-six-inch valve.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:01:59.100–00:02:10.609 (Chester Biankowski)

text: Just before you exit this compartment, look to your right at the space behind the starboard engine. There’s a narrow walkway there. Ship’s Cook Joe Agner.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:02:10.609–00:02:22.840 (Narrator)

text: And we stowed food, canned food, outboard of the engines in the engine room.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Engine Room 00:02:22.840–00:02:29.160 (Joe Eichner)

text: This is the forward engine room. It housed the other two main diesel engines. Chester Biankowski.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:00:01.019–00:00:07.739 (Narrator)

text: In the engine room, I was an oiler. An oiler is a guy that does all the paperwork, takes all the readings on the engines, checks all the machinery in the oil, runs the purifiers, makes water.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:00:07.739–00:00:19.340 (Chester Biankowski)

text: Look against the wall at the far end of the room.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:00:19.340–00:00:22.940 (Narrator)

text: You’ll see two stainless steel barrel-shaped objects, the evaporators. The sub’s fresh water had to be made by distilling seawater.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:00:22.940–00:00:32.319 (Narrator)

text: Generally, you made the water when you was on a surface only.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:00:32.319–00:00:35.719 (Chester Biankowski)

text: The number one priority for water aboard the boat was always the battery. If there was any left over, we had it for cooking and coffee, and then probably lowest on the list was shower.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:00:35.719–00:00:48.000 (Chester Biankowski)

text: We couldn’t bathe very often. Water was at a premium. Showers were not frequent. It must have been, like, ten days to two weeks.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:00:48.000–00:00:56.579 (Subvet 3)

text: We took no baths, you know?
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:00:56.579–00:00:58.500 (Subvet 4)

text: You used a minimum of water.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:00:58.500–00:01:00.259 (Subvet 3)

text: No bath. You could get a pail of water, cold water.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:01:00.259–00:01:04.199 (Subvet 4)

text: The only people on board that bathed more frequently were the, uh, motor machinist.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:01:04.199–00:01:10.019 (Subvet 3)

text: Not according to motor machinist, Chester Biankowski.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:01:10.019–00:01:13.579 (Narrator)

text: You didn’t shower all the time. The cooks and bakers, yes, they showered all the time, if we had the water.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:01:13.580–00:01:21.340 (Chester Biankowski)

text: At times, we would shower in the water comes out of the air conditioner.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:01:21.340–00:01:25.000 (Subvet 5)

text: We all smell. We’ll smell each other.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Engine Room 00:01:25.000–00:01:28.019 (Subvet 4)

text: the crew’s washroom and shower.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:00:00.100–00:00:03.600 (Narrator)

text: When we first would depart from port, we would, uh, have food stashed all over, and it wasn’t unusual to have some in the showers.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:00:03.600–00:00:14.760 (Speaker 1)

text: Let’s move on. Directly below this deck was one of the ship’s two main storage batteries. This level is the main enlisted men’s berthing area. There are thirty-six bunks in this room.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:00:14.760–00:00:26.459 (Narrator)

text: We had hot bunks. We had more men than we had bunks.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:00:26.460–00:00:30.480 (Speaker 2)

text: We had two bunks for every three men. I mean, the third man was always on watch.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:00:30.480–00:00:35.640 (Speaker 3)

text: Look between the bunks for the small square lockers built into the walls. Not a lot of storage space for two months at sea. Sailors would unzip their mattress covers and stow their Navy uniforms inside. They didn’t use them much on patrol.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:00:35.640–00:00:51.440 (Narrator)

text: Typically, you wore a T-shirt, shorts, and sandals. It was usually hot aboard the boat, so you’d dress lightly, and that was it.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:00:51.440–00:01:06.220 (Speaker 1)

text: At any time of day, you could find men in here sleeping, reading, or writing letters in their bunks. O.D. Hawkins.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:01:06.760–00:01:15.040 (Narrator)

text: Monday, October the 16th, 1944. My dearest Muriel, well, honey, as you can tell by my return address, I’ve finally gotten on a sub after all of these months, so you can probably imagine how thrilled I was.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:01:15.040–00:01:30.100 (O.D. Hawkins)

text: Well, hon, I came home to find two letters from you, and I shall sort of answer them both. Mainly, I guess, ‘cause I’m not full of any-
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:01:30.100–00:01:36.460 (Speaker 6)

text: If you shouldn’t hear from me for a month or so at a time, which is possible, don’t worry about it. I like to think no news is good news.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:01:36.460–00:01:45.160 (O.D. Hawkins)

text: These envelopes all have a censored, uh, marking on them, so he was very careful what he had to say. The expression used during that time was, uh, "Loose lips, uh, sink ships."
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:01:45.160–00:01:57.800 (Speaker 6)

text: Look up at the forward end of the room for the ship’s medical locker. It has a red cross on it.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:01:57.800–00:02:05.120 (Narrator)

text: Pampanito carried no doctor. The pharmacist’s mate took care of the crew’s health.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — After Battery 00:02:05.120–00:02:13.180 (Narrator)

text: My name is Joseph Eichner, and I was one of the cooks aboard the submarine. I worked from, uh, let’s say from five thirty in the morning till, uh, six thirty at night. Well into the night. The baker, he would bake, uh, all cakes and cookies and rolls and that sort of thing.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:00:01.020–00:00:19.580 (Joseph Eichner)

text: They say a submariner ate better than anybody in the Navy.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:00:19.580–00:00:25.280 (Speaker 2)

text: Just forward of the tables is the galley or kitchen. This is where all the food was prepared.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:00:25.280–00:00:32.119 (Narrator)

text: Breakfast is, uh, French toast or fried eggs and bacon or sausages, and then noon meal, we’d have pork chops or ham or, uh, chicken or steak. We usually had French fries, and when you fed the whole crew, eighty-five men, you had to peel a lot of potatoes. The crew that was gonna relieve the, the watch that was on ate first, and then when they finished, the crew that came off ate. Twenty-four hours a day, we had coffee.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:00:32.119–00:01:02.900 (Joseph Eichner)

text: Notice the checker and acey-deucey boards on the tables. The crew’s mess was really the only recreational area on the boat for enlisted men.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:01:02.900–00:01:13.600 (Narrator)

text: We had records that we played, records and the, and the, and the radio. That, that’s all you had. "Blue Rain," I don’t know how many times I played that song. Blue rain falling down on my window pane.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:01:13.600–00:01:25.440 (Joseph Eichner)

text: Blue rain falling down on my window pane.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:01:25.440–00:01:35.740 (Speaker 4)

text: We showed movies. We’d only have a few movies, and we’d probably show ‘em quite a few times. And cards. We’d play cards.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:01:35.740–00:01:47.300 (Joseph Eichner)

text: Hello, you fighting orphans of the Pacific-
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:01:47.300–00:01:50.020 (Tokyo Rose)

text: Oh, I remember listening to Tokyo Rose, yes. Well, she was, uh, telling us submarine sailors that, uh, so-and-so was sunk, and we’re gonna be getting you, you know.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:01:50.020–00:02:03.060 (Joseph Eichner)

text: With a grain of salt. You’d listen to anything that you could to get news, I mean, of how the war was going.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:01:53.080–00:02:08.280 (Speaker 6)

text: The first request- And Bonnie Basin.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:02:08.280–00:02:13.660 (Tokyo Rose)

text: Find the grate between the dining tables.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:02:09.380–00:02:12.200 (Narrator)

text: We only had a, a small ice box and a small, uh, refrigerator. It, it was underneath. There was a, a trapdoor there, and you had the freezer in there, too.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:02:13.660–00:02:25.010 (Joseph Eichner)

text: Let’s move on. Just before you pass through the doorway that exits this room, take a look into the galley.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:02:25.010–00:02:34.190 (Narrator)

text: If you stood right outside of the galley, and the storage room was right underneath it, below the deck. The shelves were full, and the whole well was full, all the way up to the top.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:02:34.190–00:02:47.620 (Joseph Eichner)

text: So we’ll be seeing you tomorrow, but in the meanwhile, always remember to be good, and so…
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Crews Mess 00:02:47.620–00:02:54.340 (Tokyo Rose)

text: The radio room. The top-secret coding machine, the ECM Mark II. It was used to encode and decode messages.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Radio Room 00:00:00.079–00:00:09.939 (Narrator)

text: Radar Officer William Bruckert.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Radio Room 00:00:09.939–00:00:12.099 (Narrator)

text: There’s always a radioman on watch in the radio shack. Anytime we had a contact or had sunk a ship, we would report by radio. Otherwise, you just sat there and listened and copied everything.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Radio Room 00:00:12.099–00:00:23.719 (William Bruckert)

text: The red lights are for the benefit of those who have to stand night lookout watches. So that your night vision wouldn’t be interrupted.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:00:00.980–00:00:10.580 (Subvet 1)

text: Find the panel of red and green lights on the port side of the room. This is the hull opening indicator panel, nicknamed the Christmas tree.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:00:10.580–00:00:20.850 (Narrator)

text: Oh, the Christmas tree was very important. The green lights would come on when everything was in its proper position. You had to have all green lights, of course, when you submerged.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:00:20.850–00:00:32.240 (Subvet 1)

text: Rig ship for dive. Ship rig for dive, green board.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:00:32.240–00:00:36.340 (Speaker 3)

text: To dive the submarine, vents were opened in the top of the ballast tanks. This allowed air to escape and seawater to enter through the flood ports in the bottom of the tanks.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:00:36.340–00:00:45.640 (Narrator)

text: Dive, dive. Eight heads
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:00:45.640–00:00:47.970 (Speaker 3)

text: … the boat submerged. Look at the large white wheels to the left of the Christmas tree.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:00:47.970–00:00:54.340 (Narrator)

text: Full dive on the bow planes. Fly the main down hole.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:00:54.340–00:00:55.300 (Speaker 3)

text: The planesman sat here during dives and when the boat was submerged.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:00:55.300–00:00:59.900 (Narrator)

text: The bow planes and the stern planes control the depth of the submarine when it’s underwater.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:00:59.900–00:01:05.880 (Subvet 1)

text: Above the wheels are dials and gauges.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:01:05.880–00:01:08.179 (Narrator)

text: There were angle indicators on the, on the plane controls, and there was a depth, uh, indicator that told you what would happen, uh, when you would dive.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:01:08.180–00:01:18.260 (Speaker 4)

text: If it was a normal dive, nothing, uh, slid around. If it was not normal, things slid around.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:01:18.260–00:01:29.240 (Speaker 5)

text: It would generally be about a twelve, fifteen-degree angle down. Four hundred and fifty feet was the maximum they wanted us to go.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:01:26.600–00:01:33.240 (Speaker 4)

text: We went significantly deeper than that.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:01:33.240–00:01:37.230 (Subvet 1)

text: To surface, the ballast tank vents were shut, then compressed air was blown into the tanks from storage banks, forcing the water in them back out the flood ports. As the tanks filled with air, the submarine rose to the surface. Find the ladder in the middle of the room leading up to an open hatch. This goes to the conning tower. You can see the pump room through the grating in the floor by the ladder. Look around the room if you’d like. Alarms were an everyday part of life aboard. The collision alarm meant a collision was imminent. The general alarm was the call to battle stations, but the most important alarm was the dive alarm.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:01:37.230–00:02:31.360 (Narrator)

text: Whenever there was an occasion to call for the ship to dive, the officer of the deck would sound the diving alarm, and you’ll clear the bridge and dive, dive.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:02:31.360–00:02:40.060 (Speaker 6)

text: Clear the bridge. Dive, dive!
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:02:40.060–00:02:41.660 (Speaker 3)

text: You didn’t go down soft and easy. You went down fast.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:02:41.660–00:02:46.200 (Speaker 7)

text: The three lookouts and the officer of the deck had to get from your perch up on the bridge, down to the ladder that goes to the conning tower, down the ladder to the control room within thirty seconds.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:02:46.200–00:03:02.360 (Subvet 1)

text: We tried to get to periscope depth, which would be sixty feet, in thirty seconds.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Control Room 00:03:02.360–00:03:07.580 (Speaker 7)

text: The conning tower was the control center for navigation and battle. From here, enemy targets were tracked and sunk.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Conning Tower 00:00:00.319–00:00:07.599 (Speaker 0)

text: The SJ radar was located in the conning tower.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Conning Tower 00:00:07.599–00:00:10.940 (Speaker 1)

text: The sonar equipment controls are up in the conning tower.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Conning Tower 00:00:10.940–00:00:13.559 (Speaker 2)

text: The torpedo data computer, the two periscopes, the helm — this is the conning tower, the battle control center. The TDC is a mechanical analog computer that calculated firing solutions: it tracked the target speed, range, and bearing, then automatically set the correct gyroscope angle on each torpedo before launch. Without the TDC, accurately hitting a moving ship from a submerged submarine would have been nearly impossible.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Conning Tower 00:00:13.559–00:00:16.719 (Speaker 1)

text: The, uh, steering wheel, the helm.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Conning Tower 00:00:16.719–00:00:18.619 (Speaker 3)

text: Sometimes the area is referred to as officer’s country.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:00:00.740–00:00:04.460 (Subvet 1)

text: The ship’s office.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:00:04.460–00:00:06.280 (Narrator)

text: I’m Charlie McGuire, first class and chief yeoman. Chief yeoman is the ship’s clerk. See, I knew everybody aboard.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:00:06.280–00:00:15.020 (Charlie McGuire)

text: The yeoman probably knew more than the officers did. The yeoman typed everything that went out of that ship, and he read all the official mail that came in.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:00:15.020–00:00:22.840 (Subvet 1)

text: And I was quite a typist. It’s a very good rate, yeoman.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:00:22.840–00:00:28.020 (Charlie McGuire)

text: Step forward into the hallway. This area housed eight officers, plus the executive and commanding officers. Just ahead are their staterooms. Radar Officer Richard Sherlock.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:00:28.020–00:00:40.160 (Narrator)

text: The big thing was developing, I think, a sense of confidence among the crew that you were competent as an officer.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:00:40.160–00:00:48.620 (Richard Sherlock)

text: On your right, you’ll see Commanding Officer Pete Summers’ stateroom. The CO had a writing desk, deep depth gauge, and a repeater compass, so he could track the sub’s course at all times. Further along on the left is the wardroom, where officers ate, met, and passed the little free time they had. Richard Sherlock:
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:00:48.620–00:01:09.400 (Narrator)

text: I don’t remember there being a cross word between the officers. Uh, most of us back and forth used our first names. I’m sure the crew had nicknames for all of us that they didn’t use in our presence.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:01:09.400–00:01:23.430 (Richard Sherlock)

text: My name is George Ingram. They called me The Kid, The Good Kid.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:01:23.430–00:01:30.460 (George Ingram)

text: At the far end, on the left, is a small pantry for the steward’s mate.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:01:30.460–00:01:35.520 (Narrator)

text: As a steward mate, you served the officers food. You really had to know how to set the table.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:01:35.520–00:01:42.660 (George Ingram)

text: The officers ate the same food that the men ate. The steward would come in and, uh, take what he needed for the officers and bring it up there.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:01:42.660–00:01:50.740 (Charlie McGuire)

text: They all ate the same food, except you could give them a little special. I would cook some bacon and dice some eggs, and make my own thousand island dressing.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Battery 00:01:50.740–00:02:04.580 (George Ingram)

text: On either side of door are steel posts. These allowed the sonar heads to be lowered down below the hull into the water for submerged listening. Step into the room. The sonarmen would be the first to hear an approaching enemy ship.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:00:00.080–00:00:16.160 (Narrator)

text: We were severely depth charged, you know, on the one patrol. We had a sound operator, and he had his headset on, and he could listen.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:00:16.160–00:00:23.900 (Subvet 1)

text: You could hear ships, like, thirty thousand yards away or so.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:00:23.900–00:00:28.060 (Subvet 2)

text: And he’d say, "They’re pinging on us." In other words, they were sending a sound.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:00:28.060–00:00:31.480 (Subvet 1)

text: When they’d ping, the sonar would come down and bounce off.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:00:31.480–00:00:35.200 (Subvet 2)

text: When they bounce it off you, they know you’re there.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:00:35.200–00:00:38.440 (Subvet 3)

text: This guy would be speaking in the phone, you know, "They’re pinging on us. They’re making a run." And then you could hear the big propellers of that ship above, you know?
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:00:38.440–00:00:48.660 (Subvet 1)

text: The boat would be rigged for what they call silent running. Everything was turned off that could be turned off. You had no air conditioning, uh, fans, or anything like that.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:00:48.660–00:01:00.020 (Subvet 4)

text: The humidity would immediately go over a hundred percent. The perspiration just ran on you.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:00.020–00:01:05.620 (Subvet 5)

text: I’ve never been really frightened.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:05.620–00:01:07.479 (Subvet 6)

text: If they’re gonna get you, they’re gonna get you.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:07.480–00:01:15.890 (Subvet 3)

text: They dropped, I think, five depth charges, which were extremely close. The sound was indescribable, it was so loud.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:15.890–00:01:25.820 (Subvet 7)

text: You heard a real thump. I mean, a real thump.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:25.820–00:01:28.660 (Subvet 6)

text: One hell of a clank.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:28.660–00:01:30.020 (Subvet 4)

text: The, uh, light bulbs shattered. The, the cork that the boat was lined with flew off the walls.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:30.020–00:01:37.520 (Subvet 7)

text: It darn near got us.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:37.520–00:01:38.820 (Subvet 3)

text: That old statement about there not being, uh, atheists in foxholes, it’s true. There aren’t any atheists under depth charge, either.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:38.820–00:01:46.240 (Subvet 5)

text: You pray. Everybody has a maker you pray to.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:46.240–00:01:53.180 (Subvet 6)

text: Look up. The large hatch overhead, in the center of the compartment, is the escape trunk. It was designed to permit the crew to escape if the submarine were disabled in shallow water.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:01:54.560–00:02:09.900 (Narrator)

text: You’ve probably concluded by now that it was tight quarters aboard a submarine. Now, try to imagine this boat with seventy-three extra men. That’s how many British and Australian prisoners of war Pampanito rescued from the South China Sea in September 1944. Pampanito had been part of a submarine wolf pack that had attacked a Japanese convoy and sunk several ships. Unbeknownst to the submarines, the ships had been carrying Allied prisoners.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Torpedo Room 00:02:09.900–00:02:34.020 (Narrator)

text: We’re now on the forward part of the submarine. Pampanito’s guns, including the large wet mount gun to your left, were made to be submerged.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:00:02.920–00:00:12.220 (Narrator)

text: Look up at the bridge. The periscope shears are the tall, gray structures. On either side of them, surrounded by metal railings, are the lookout stands.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:00:12.220–00:00:21.780 (Narrator)

text: When you’re on the surface, you’re out looking for whatever may be in any way a hazard to the ship.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:00:21.780–00:00:28.939 (Joe Senneff)

text: USS Threadfin Seaman Second Class Joe Senneff:
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:00:28.940–00:00:32.460 (Narrator)

text: In heavy seas, the lookout would have to brace his body against the steel rail that surrounds the lookout platform to keep from being washed overboard if it was really rough weather.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:00:32.460–00:00:42.040 (Joe Senneff)

text: Look at the broom on top of the periscope shears.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:00:42.040–00:00:45.120 (Narrator)

text: The broom that you see at the top of the periscope shears was used to indicate to people in port that you had had a successful war patrol, a clean sweep.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:00:45.120–00:00:54.540 (Joe Senneff)

text: Look out at the bay. Imagine gazing out over the open sea for hours on end. Gordon Hopper describes the first lookout sighting of the prisoners of war Pampanito rescued.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:00:54.540–00:01:06.760 (Narrator)

text: We began seeing these men on, uh, makeshift rafts-
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:01:06.760–00:01:11.080 (Gordon Hopper)

text: Boards and oil
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:01:11.080–00:01:12.120 (Australian 1)

text: … anything that would float, really. [chuckles] We began to see them off in the distance, and the captain and the lookouts all thought that these were Japanese survivors from the ships that we had sunk.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:01:12.120–00:01:24.720 (Gordon Hopper)

text: Two rafts with men on them!
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:01:24.720–00:01:26.920 (Australian 1)

text: As we got up close to this closest raft, this big person stood up and started waving this large Australian hat and calling out. I still remember what he said. He says, "Oh, you bloody Yanks, one day you bloody well sink us, the next day you bloody well pick us up."
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:01:26.920–00:01:44.770 (Gordon Hopper)

text: "You sink us, then you save us, you bloody Yanks."
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:01:44.770–00:01:47.110 (Australian 1)

text: "Who are you?" "Prisoners of war. Australians, British. Pick us up, please!" "Take them aboard."
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:01:47.110–00:01:55.020 (Australian 1)

text: Look at the submarine’s rounded hull.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:01:55.020–00:01:56.820 (Narrator)

text: Make way, make way.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:01:56.820–00:01:57.970 (Australian 1)

text: It wasn’t easy to get the survivors over it and up onto the deck.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:01:57.970–00:02:02.360 (Narrator)

text: There were about five or six of us who were among the better swimmers, who, uh, would swim out to the rafts with a line and pull them in and get these men off the rafts and onto the boat and down below, cleaned up. They had been, uh, in the water for four days. They were coated with, uh, crude oil.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:02:02.360–00:02:24.580 (Gordon Hopper)

text: Pampanito sped to Saipan, the nearest American base. Only one man was lost on the way. Let’s move aft toward the gangway.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:02:24.580–00:02:33.720 (Narrator)

text: When they got off the ship, they were grinning from ear to ear. They looked like seventy-two US sailors going on shore leave.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:02:33.720–00:02:43.160 (O.D. Hawkins)

text: Most of us felt that was the highlight of our, uh, time in the service, was being able to save these men.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:02:43.160–00:02:50.350 (Gordon Hopper)

text: "I wish to thank the officers and men of this sub for saving myself and fellow men from the deep. God bless them all, R.F. Granger."
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:02:50.350–00:02:58.290 (Australian 1)

text: The Japanese have just officially laid down their arms.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:03:00.030–00:03:04.579 (Harry Truman)

text: President Harry Truman announcing the Japanese surrender, September 1st, 1945.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:03:04.580–00:03:10.640 (Narrator)

text: They have signed terms of unconditional surrender.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:03:10.640–00:03:16.100 (Harry Truman)

text: Four years ago, the thoughts and fears of the whole civilized world were centered on another piece of American soil, Pearl Harbor.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:03:16.100–00:03:27.320 (Harry Truman)

text: The mighty threat to civilization, which began there, is now laid at rest.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:03:27.320–00:03:33.120 (Harry Truman)

text: During World War II, Pampanito was credited with sinking six enemy ships. Captain Edward Beach.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:03:33.120–00:03:39.620 (Narrator)

text: Submarines like the Pampanito played a critical and decisive role during World War II. They sank over twelve hundred Japanese merchant ships and warships, more than half of all Japanese shipping destroyed in the war. When the rate of sinkings outpaced Japan’s ability to build new ships, the outcome of the war was assured.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:03:39.620–00:03:59.710 (Edward Beach)

text: But this success was not without its cost.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:00.260–00:04:03.640 (Edward Beach)

text: Fifty-two of our submarines never returned. Most of them disappeared without trace, their fate unknown. All but a few of their crews lie forever entombed on the ocean floor. Three thousand five hundred and five men and officers of the silent service gave their lives to the cause of victory.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:04.120–00:04:22.770 (Edward Beach)

text: The Pampanito stands as one of a few proud memorials- … to these men still on patrol. On behalf of the Pampanito, thank you for joining me today.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:22.820–00:04:34.159 (Edward Beach)

text: Thanks to the USS Pampanito volunteers, veterans, and others who made this tour possible. Naval Reserve USS Frank Cable, AS-40, Detachment N, Sacramento.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:34.160–00:04:46.880 (Narrator)

text: Robert Bennett.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:46.880–00:04:47.700 (Australian 1)

text: William Brockert.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:47.700–00:04:48.700 (Joe Senneff)

text: Joseph Eitner.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:48.700–00:04:49.700 (Joe Senneff)

text: O.D. Hawkins.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:49.700–00:04:50.740 (O.D. Hawkins)

text: Gordon Hopper.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:50.740–00:04:52.100 (Gordon Hopper)

text: George Ingram.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:52.100–00:04:54.520 (George Ingram)

text: Charlie Maguire.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:54.520–00:04:55.800 (Charlie Maguire)

text: George Moffitt.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:55.800–00:04:56.920 (Australian 1)

text: Joe Senneff.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:56.920–00:04:57.980 (Joe Senneff)

text: Richard Sherlock.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:57.980–00:04:59.100 (Joe Senneff)

text: Ted Swain.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:04:59.100–00:05:00.780 (Australian 1)

text: Bob Taylor.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:05:00.780–00:05:02.140 (Joe Senneff)

text: And Chester Biankoski.
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:05:02.140–00:05:04.060 (Narrator)

text: Let’s remember Pearl Harbor, and go on to victory!
display_citation: Pampanito Audio Tour — Forward Deck 00:05:04.060–00:05:23.320 (Speaker 12)
